preFix.title: Selecting fibre for yarn weight
preFix.subtitle: Matching breed, prep, and staple length to your intended weight
preFix.excerpt: Choosing the right fibre for a planned yarn weight involves more than picking a breed. Micron count, staple length, preparation type, and the minimum-length rule for short-draw all affect what weight comes off the wheel. This guide maps those variables to outcome and includes a summary of common breeds by typical handspun weight.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from published handspinning fibre selection references, breed guides, and fleece-to-yarn instruction books. Micron and staple length ranges are representative published figures. Breed-to-weight mapping reflects standard published guidance. No single public domain text.

content.text: The yarn weight you can spin from a given fibre is not set by breed or
micron count
alone.
Staple length

, preparation type, and drafting technique all contribute. A coarse-micron fibre can be spun fine if the preparation aligns it well and the spinner drafts carefully; a fine-micron fibre with very short staple may be difficult to spin thin because the fibres keep slipping out of the draft zone. Understanding each variable separately makes fibre selection much more deliberate.
Micron count and spinning fineness

Micron count measures fibre diameter. An 18-micron Merino is much finer than a 33-micron Swaledale. Finer fibre can be spun thinner, because fewer fibres fill each unit of yarn diameter. But micron count and spinning fineness are not the same thing. A 28-micron Bluefaced Leicester in combed tops spins finer than an 18-micron Merino in batt form, because preparation matters as much as raw fibre diameter. Micron count sets the softness limit. Preparation and technique decide how fine you can spin.
The staple length rule for short-draw
In short-draw spinning, the
draft window

is the gap between the two hands during drafting. For fibres to draft cleanly without slipping free, each individual fibre must bridge the full draft window with some length to spare. The standard minimum is that the staple must be at least three times the draft window length. For a 10 cm draft window, staple must be at least 30 cm; for a 3 cm draft window, staple of 9 cm is sufficient.

This rule explains why very short-staple fibres are harder to spin fine: to keep the draft window small enough for the staple to bridge it, you must hold your hands very close together, which limits the thickness you can draft. Cotton (staple 1-3 cm) requires a supported long-draw for this reason. Extremely fine-stapled Merino at under 6 cm behaves similarly.
Tops, roving, batt, and combed locks
Commercial
tops

have the fibres fully aligned in parallel, which allows smooth, consistent drafting. They spin easily into fine and medium-fine yarn but produce a denser, less lofty result than an airy batt. Tops are the go-to preparation for worsted-style spinning.
Commercial
roving

is similar to tops but with less alignment: the fibres are mostly parallel with some crossover. It drafts easily and suits beginners who want a slightly more forgiving hand feel than tops. The yarn it produces is slightly softer and airier than tops-spun yarn at the same weight.
A
batt

is a sheet of randomly oriented carded fibre. It is the best preparation for a woollen-style, lofty, air-filled yarn. Batts are harder to spin fine because the random fibre orientation creates resistance in the draft zone. They produce beautiful chunky and aran-weight yarn with a soft, cloud-like texture, and they are the standard preparation for thick-and-thin art yarns.

Combed locks sit between combed tops and raw fleece: the fibres are aligned by hand combing, with VM removed, but the locks retain more of the breed's natural character than commercially processed tops. They suit lock-spinning and supported long-draw, and produce semi-lustrous yarn with clear breed character.
Breed to typical handspun weight: a summary
The list below is a guide, not a rule. A skilled spinner can push any breed thinner or thicker. These are the weights most commonly produced from each breed with normal effort.
Jacob (micron 30-38, staple 7-12 cm): chunky to aran. Short staple and coarser micron count suit thick-and-thin and natural-coloured art yarn.
Merino (micron 15-24, staple 6-10 cm): lace to DK. The standard choice for fine spinning; fine-to-medium tops are widely available and spin smoothly.
Bluefaced Leicester (micron 24-28, staple 10-15 cm): fingering to DK. Long staple and semi-lustre make it a versatile breed for fine-to-medium weight yarn.
Teeswater (micron 30-36, staple 20-30 cm): bulky to chunky art yarn and lock-spun statement singles. The very long, lustrous locks are suited to textural and art yarn work rather than fine spinning.
Oxford Down (micron 27-32, staple 7-10 cm): DK to aran. Soft and forgiving; a standard teaching breed and a reliable everyday garment yarn.
Dorset Horn (micron 26-30, staple 7-10 cm): fingering to DK. Good crimp gives the yarn elasticity, making it one of the better British breeds for sock weight.

When selecting fibre for a project, start with the weight you need, then look for a breed whose typical range includes it, and then choose the preparation that suits your drafting style. Tops for fine and consistent; roving for medium and forgiving; batt for lofty and chunky.
